question: How do you set up CI/CD for Flutter with GitHub Actions?
answer:
CI/CD pipeline structure:

CI (every PR):
1. Checkout → setup Flutter → pub get
2. dart format --check
3. flutter analyze
4. flutter test --coverage
5. Upload coverage to Codecov

CD (merge to main/release):
1. Build signed artifact (APK/IPA)
2. Upload to Firebase App Distribution (beta) or Play Store/App Store (prod)

Secrets management: Store keystore, certificates, API keys as GitHub Secrets. Never commit them.

Fastlane: Automate signing, versioning, screenshots, and store submissions.
code_example:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: Flutter CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with:
          flutter-version: '3.22.0'
          cache: true

      - run: flutter pub get

      - name: Check formatting
        run: dart format --output=none --set-exit-if-changed .

      - name: Analyze
        run: flutter analyze --fatal-infos

      - name: Test with coverage
        run: flutter test --coverage

      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          file: coverage/lcov.info

  build-android:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0', cache: true }

      - name: Decode keystore
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_B64 }}" | base64 --decode > android/app/release.jks

      - name: Build release APK
        env:
          STORE_PASS: ${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_PASS }}
          KEY_ALIAS: ${{ secrets.KEY_ALIAS }}
          KEY_PASS: ${{ secrets.KEY_PASS }}
        run: flutter build apk --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=debug-info/

      - name: Deploy to Firebase App Distribution
        uses: wzieba/Firebase-Distribution-Github-Action@v1
        with:
          appId: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_APP_ID }}
          token: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_TOKEN }}
          groups: beta-testers
          file: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk
          releaseNotes: "Build from commit ${{ github.sha }}"

question: What are Flutter flavors and how do you set up dev/staging/production environments?
answer:
Flavors (called "schemes" on iOS, "build types/product flavors" on Android) let you build multiple variants of your app from the same codebase — with different:
• Bundle IDs / package names
• API endpoints
• App names and icons
• Firebase projects
• Feature flags

This enables separate dev/staging/prod apps installable side-by-side.

Setup steps:
1. Configure Android productFlavors in build.gradle
2. Configure iOS schemes in Xcode
3. Create Dart entry points per flavor (or use --dart-define)
4. Use flutter_flavorizr package to automate the setup

question: How do you sign Android and iOS builds in CI without leaking the keys?
answer:
Signing is the single highest-stakes secret in mobile CI. Lose the upload key and you can't publish updates; leak the .p12 and someone can ship a malicious update under your identity. The discipline is: keys stay encrypted at rest, decoded at build time into a temporary file, and shredded when the job ends.

Android:
• Generate a release keystore (.jks) once, store it Base64-encoded in GitHub Secrets / your CI's secret store
• A keystore.properties file holds storeFile, storePassword, keyAlias, keyPassword — generated on the fly in CI from secrets
• `signingConfigs.release` in build.gradle reads from keystore.properties
• Use Play App Signing — let Google hold the upload key. You sign with an upload key, Google re-signs with the actual app signing key. Lose the upload key and you can recover via Play Console.

iOS:
• Two artifacts: a Distribution Certificate (.p12) and a Provisioning Profile (.mobileprovision)
• fastlane match is the standard — store both, encrypted, in a private git repo; CI calls `fastlane match appstore --readonly`
• Alternative: store .p12 + profile as Base64 in CI secrets, decode in a temp keychain that's deleted after build

Always:
• Mark the secrets as masked in CI output
• Restrict who can read or rotate them; keep an audit log
• Rotate after any team member with access leaves
• Do not commit keystore.properties or .p12 — put them in .gitignore explicitly
code_example:
# .github/workflows/release.yml — Android signing
jobs:
  build-android:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }

      - name: Decode keystore
        run: echo "${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_BASE64 }}" | base64 --decode > android/app/release.jks

      - name: Write keystore.properties
        run: |
          cat > android/key.properties <<EOF
          storeFile=release.jks
          storePassword=${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_PASSWORD }}
          keyAlias=${{ secrets.KEY_ALIAS }}
          keyPassword=${{ secrets.KEY_PASSWORD }}
          EOF

      - name: Build app bundle
        run: flutter build appbundle --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/

      - name: Cleanup
        if: always()
        run: |
          rm -f android/app/release.jks android/key.properties

# build.gradle reads the properties
# def keystoreProps = new Properties()
# keystoreProps.load(new FileInputStream(rootProject.file("key.properties")))
# signingConfigs { release { storeFile file(keystoreProps['storeFile']); ... } }

# ── iOS: fastlane match — recommended ────────────
# Fastfile
# lane :ci_release do
#   setup_ci   # creates a temporary keychain
#   match(type: 'appstore', readonly: true,
#         git_url: 'git@github.com:acme/match-certs.git')
#   build_app(scheme: 'Runner', export_method: 'app-store')
# end

# CI step:
#   - run: bundle exec fastlane ios ci_release
#     env:
#       MATCH_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.MATCH_PASSWORD }}
#       APP_STORE_CONNECT_API_KEY_KEY: ${{ secrets.APP_STORE_API_KEY }}
#       MATCH_KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD }}

question: How do you manage app versioning in Flutter — version, build number, semver?
answer:
Three numbers travel together in every build: the public version, the build number, and the semantic intent of the change. They serve different audiences.

pubspec.yaml `version` field — `1.4.2+87`:
• `1.4.2` is the public version (semver-style: major.minor.patch). Visible in stores, in About screens.
• `+87` is the build number — strictly increasing integer. Stores require it to be higher than any previously uploaded build.
• Flutter expands this into Android `versionName`/`versionCode` and iOS `CFBundleShortVersionString`/`CFBundleVersion`

Rules to live by:
• Build number must always go up. The cleanest source is the CI build counter (GitHub Actions `${{ github.run_number }}`) or a monotonic timestamp
• Don't let two CI runs produce the same build number — even on a re-run
• Public version follows semver intent: bump patch for fixes, minor for additive features, major for breaking UX/API changes that users care about
• On hotfixes branched from a tagged release, bump only the patch and the build number — don't accidentally release `1.5.0+old` ahead of `1.4.2`

Driving it from one source:
• Manual: edit pubspec.yaml on every release. Simple, fragile.
• `flutter build --build-name=1.4.2 --build-number=$RUN_NUMBER` overrides pubspec at build time. Recommended for CI.
• Generated build-info file in code so the running app can show its own version (`PackageInfo.fromPlatform()`).
code_example:
# pubspec.yaml
version: 1.4.2+87
# major.minor.patch+build_number
#  ↓             ↓
#  Android versionName     versionCode = 87
#  iOS CFBundleShortVer    CFBundleVersion

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release
on:
  push:
    tags: ['v*']      # e.g. v1.4.2 triggers a release build

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }

      - name: Derive version
        id: ver
        run: |
          # Strip leading 'v' from the tag
          VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
          BUILD="${{ github.run_number }}"
          echo "version=$VERSION"   >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "build=$BUILD"       >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build (override pubspec values)
        run: |
          flutter build appbundle --release \
            --build-name=${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }} \
            --build-number=${{ steps.ver.outputs.build }} \
            --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/

# ── Read version inside the app at runtime ──────────
# import 'package:package_info_plus/package_info_plus.dart';
#
# final info = await PackageInfo.fromPlatform();
# print('${info.version}+${info.buildNumber}');  // 1.4.2+87

question: How do you automate Play Store and App Store releases from CI?
answer:
Both stores expose APIs for unattended uploads. Reaching them safely from CI is the standard final step of a release pipeline.

Google Play:
• Generate a service account in Google Cloud, grant it Play Console access
• Download its JSON key — store as a CI secret
• Use `fastlane supply` or the Gradle `play-publisher` plugin to upload an .aab and bump tracks (internal → alpha → beta → production)
• Tracks let you stage rollouts: upload to internal first, promote when smoke tests pass, then ramp production from 1% → 10% → 100%

Apple App Store:
• Generate an App Store Connect API Key (.p8 + key id + issuer id) — replaces username/password / 2FA pain
• Use `fastlane pilot` to upload to TestFlight, `deliver` to push metadata + binary to App Store, `submit_to_app_review` to start review
• TestFlight build availability typically takes 5–30 minutes after upload
• App Store metadata (screenshots, descriptions, what's new) can be checked into the repo (fastlane metadata) so each release ships with version-controlled copy

Guardrails:
• Always upload to internal/TestFlight first; gate prod promotion on a manual approval step or a green smoke job
• On a build pulled from review, your CI should be able to re-promote a previous artifact rather than rebuild — keep .aab/.ipa artifacts retained for at least 90 days
• Never let a CI matrix accidentally upload the same build to production twice; idempotency check on the build number is cheap insurance
code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile
default_platform(:android)

platform :android do
  desc 'Upload AAB to Play Console internal track'
  lane :internal do
    upload_to_play_store(
      track: 'internal',
      aab: '../build/app/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab',
      json_key_data: ENV['PLAY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON'],
      release_status: 'completed',
    )
  end

  desc 'Promote internal → production with staged rollout'
  lane :promote do |options|
    upload_to_play_store(
      track: 'internal', track_promote_to: 'production',
      rollout: options[:rollout] || '0.1',  # 10%
      json_key_data: ENV['PLAY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON'],
    )
  end
end

platform :ios do
  desc 'Upload IPA to TestFlight'
  lane :beta do
    api_key = app_store_connect_api_key(
      key_id: ENV['ASC_KEY_ID'],
      issuer_id: ENV['ASC_ISSUER_ID'],
      key_content: ENV['ASC_API_KEY_P8'],
    )
    upload_to_testflight(
      api_key: api_key,
      ipa: '../build/ios/ipa/Runner.ipa',
      skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true,
    )
  end
end

# .github/workflows/release.yml — release stage
# - run: bundle exec fastlane android internal
# - run: bundle exec fastlane ios beta
# - name: Manual approval for prod ramp
#   uses: trstringer/manual-approval@v1
#   with: { approvers: alice,bob }
# - run: bundle exec fastlane android promote rollout:0.1

question: How do you set up beta distribution with Firebase App Distribution and TestFlight?
answer:
Beta channels are how you get a build into the hands of real users without going through the public store review and ramp. Each platform has a different best-practice channel.

iOS — TestFlight (Apple-only, no alternative):
• Internal testers: up to 100, no review needed, instant access — invite by Apple ID
• External testers: up to 10,000, requires Apple's beta review (~24h on first build, automatic on subsequent ones in the same group)
• Build expires after 90 days, so testers always have a fresh artifact
• Crash reports flow into Xcode Organizer / your APM

Android — Firebase App Distribution OR Play Console internal/closed testing:
• Firebase App Distribution: by-email invitations, OTA updates, supports APKs (faster turnaround for ad-hoc testing)
• Play Console internal track: tied to the store identity — closer to what users actually get; required if you want Play Integrity attestations to work in beta
• Many teams run both — Firebase for engineering smoke tests, Play internal for product/QA

Process hygiene:
• Auto-publish every main-branch build to a private internal channel — catches build issues before they touch a tester
• Promote tagged release builds to a wider beta group; keep release notes per build
• Wire crash reports / analytics back to a build_id so you can correlate beta crashes to commits
• Have a recovery path: if a beta is broken, you can roll back testers to an older artifact without going through a full release
code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile — Firebase App Distribution
platform :android do
  desc 'Distribute APK to internal smoke channel'
  lane :smoke do
    firebase_app_distribution(
      app: ENV['FIREBASE_ANDROID_APP_ID'],   # 1:NNN:android:xxx
      service_credentials_file: 'firebase-cd.json',
      android_artifact_path: '../build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk',
      android_artifact_type: 'APK',
      groups: 'engineering-smoke',
      release_notes_file: 'release_notes.txt',
    )
  end
end

# .github/workflows/beta.yml
name: Beta
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  android-beta:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - run: flutter build apk --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/
      - name: Distribute to Firebase
        uses: wzieba/Firebase-Distribution-Github-Action@v1
        with:
          appId: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_ANDROID_APP_ID }}
          serviceCredentialsFileContent: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_SA_JSON }}
          groups: engineering-smoke
          file: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk
          releaseNotes: |
            Build: ${{ github.sha }}
            Branch: ${{ github.ref_name }}

  ios-testflight:
    runs-on: macos-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - name: Build IPA
        run: flutter build ipa --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/
      - name: Upload to TestFlight
        uses: apple-actions/upload-testflight-build@v1
        with:
          app-path: build/ios/ipa/Runner.ipa
          issuer-id: ${{ secrets.ASC_ISSUER_ID }}
          api-key-id: ${{ secrets.ASC_KEY_ID }}
          api-private-key: ${{ secrets.ASC_API_KEY_P8 }}

question: How do you avoid flaky integration tests in Flutter CI?
answer:
Integration tests on emulators in CI are notorious for flakes that block green builds for reasons unrelated to your code. The solution is a mix of test design (eliminate timing assumptions) and infra design (eliminate environment surprises).

Design the tests:
• Never `Future.delayed` to wait for UI — use `await tester.pumpAndSettle()` for animations, or pump until a finder appears with a polling helper. Sleeps are the #1 source of flake.
• Replace network with deterministic test doubles. The test should not depend on a real backend, ever. Use a recorded fixture or an in-process fake server (`shelf` works well).
• Stub time. If your code reads `DateTime.now()` or starts timers, expose a Clock abstraction and inject a fake; otherwise tests behave differently at midnight.
• Make IDs stable. Use Keys for finder-by-id rather than text. Translations and rebuilds break text-based finders.
• Reset state between tests. Wipe localStorage/secure storage in setUp; set a fresh ProviderScope (Riverpod) or recreate the Cubits.

Design the infra:
• Use one device per test, not parallel devices on the same emulator — emulators sharing GPU on CI runners stutter
• Boot the emulator with `-no-snapshot-save -no-window -no-boot-anim -gpu swiftshader_indirect` for headless reliability
• Pre-warm: open a dummy Flutter app once before running the suite to avoid first-build cold-start variance
• Quarantine flakes: tag a test `@Tags(['flaky'])` and run it nightly with retries instead of blocking PRs
• Capture screenshots and logs on failure — re-running blindly hides flake patterns

question: What is fastlane and what does it actually buy you over a hand-rolled CI script?
answer:
fastlane is a Ruby tool that turns the long, error-prone sequences of cert/profile/build/upload commands into named lanes. You write a Fastfile once, then `fastlane release` does the right thing on any machine — local, CI, or a teammate's laptop.

What lanes wrap:
• `match` — synchronizes signing certs and provisioning profiles across the team via an encrypted git repo
• `gym` — builds an iOS .ipa with the right scheme, configuration, export options
• `pilot` / `upload_to_testflight` — uploads to TestFlight and waits for processing
• `supply` / `upload_to_play_store` — uploads .aab/.apk to Play and manages tracks
• `deliver` — manages App Store metadata, screenshots, what's new
• `screengrab` / `snapshot` — automated localized screenshots

What you actually buy:
• Reproducibility: any clean machine with the right secrets can produce the same artifact
• Auditability: lanes are checked into the repo, code-reviewed like any other code
• Smaller-blast-radius secrets: fastlane can use App Store Connect API keys (no human 2FA), match (read-only mode in CI)
• Recovery patterns: lanes that re-promote already-built artifacts, lanes that roll back, lanes that bisect a regression by version

When NOT to bother:
• A solo project shipping monthly — bash + `flutter build` + a couple of GitHub actions is enough
• If you're already on Bitrise/Codemagic that abstracts this — adding fastlane doubles the layers without much benefit
code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile
default_platform(:ios)

before_all do
  ensure_git_status_clean
  ensure_git_branch(branch: 'main')
end

platform :ios do
  desc 'Sync signing — read-only in CI'
  lane :certs do
    match(type: 'appstore', readonly: ENV['CI'] == 'true')
  end

  desc 'Build, archive, upload to TestFlight'
  lane :beta do
    certs
    increment_build_number(build_number: ENV['CI_BUILD_NUMBER'] || latest_testflight_build_number + 1)
    build_app(
      scheme: 'Runner',
      export_method: 'app-store',
      output_directory: 'build/ios/ipa',
    )
    upload_to_testflight(
      skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true,
    )
  end

  desc 'Promote latest TestFlight build to App Store, with phased release'
  lane :release do
    deliver(
      submit_for_review: true,
      automatic_release: false,
      phased_release: true,                 # 1% / 2% / 5% / 10% / 20% / 50% / 100%
      force: true,                          # skip metadata HTML preview
      precheck_include_in_app_purchases: true,
      submission_information: { add_id_info_uses_idfa: false },
    )
  end
end

# Usage
# bundle exec fastlane ios beta             # nightly
# bundle exec fastlane ios release          # tagged release
# Override env: ASC_KEY_ID / ASC_ISSUER_ID / ASC_API_KEY_P8 set in CI

question: How do you do staged rollouts and quick rollbacks safely?
answer:
Mobile releases ship to thousands of devices in minutes once you push the button. A staged rollout caps the blast radius if something is broken; a clean rollback path lets you stop bleeding fast.

Staged rollouts:
• Play Console — production track supports percentage rollouts (1% → 5% → 20% → 50% → 100%). Bake at each stage for at least 24h while watching crashlytics + key metrics.
• App Store — Phased Release ramps 1/2/5/10/20/50/100% over 7 days. You can pause from App Store Connect at any stage.
• Both let you halt and resume. Use the pause button when in doubt — the cost of a 12h delay is negligible.

What to monitor during a rollout:
• Crash-free user rate vs. previous version (set a guardrail: <0.5% drop is fine, >1% drop pause)
• Critical funnel metrics: sign-in success, checkout completion, push opt-in
• Backend errors that spiked with the rollout
• Reviews / support tickets in real time

Rollback options (in order of preference):
• Halt the staged rollout — Play Console immediately stops new users from getting the bad build; Phased Release pauses the ramp
• Server-side feature flag — hide the bad feature without an app update. Always ship risky features behind a remote flag.
• Re-release the previous build with a new version number — slow (review queue), but works. Keep the previous artifact warm in CI for 30+ days.
• Force-update screen — the app calls a config endpoint that says "please update". The current build still runs but a soft block escalates to hard if needed.

Guardrail: ship every release behind at least one feature flag for the new code paths. "Roll back" by toggling the flag is 1000x faster and safer than re-releasing.
